sentences.text: On the 8th of May, 1945.
A Russian soldier, one.
Come on the horse, without saddle.
And-- the war is over, he called-- he called, but in Russian.
And all of a sudden, he saw someone and yells, he was [INAUDIBLE].
And the next day, or the same day, came a-- captain of the Russian army.
And it will be very safe, but I am-- I am-- [INAUDIBLE].
But don't tell anybody because they will shoot me from the behind.
The Russian soldier was Jewish.
Yeah.
This-- this man was Jewish.
And he was very helpful.
We get some bread, and something, so it was very helpful.
No.
Oh, Schindler ran away the day before.
He proposed me to, to-- he gave me some revolvers.
[INAUDIBLE] with very heavy draw.
But I told him, Mr.
Schindler, I have a big family.
I couldn't leave my wife and my mother ill.
I have to take care of them.
He understand it well.
Many other people had been running towards the proposition because he-- they have a very-- [INAUDIBLE] for Switzerland, for Switzerland.
And he was accompanied by four people.
The driver was [INAUDIBLE].
I couldn't remember.
I'll probably remember it later.
There was one girl from Cracow, and another boy, who is living now in Argentina.
They were Jewish.
And they went with Schindler to Switzerland.
Schindler.
Schindler was-- I have a-- he wanted to be secure, because, I believe, the German and Czechoslovakian people were against him, you know?
Because he-- he take a German list, and so on.
So he wanted to be sure.
Some kind of security, yes, the Jews from the camp, and so he was Jewish as well.
No.
Oh, yes.
He make an appeal.
And we were together.
And we had a very nice [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] talk.
He asked us not to take the law in our own hands.
Only when the war is finished, the-- the right court will deal with it.
No.
I wished him very well.
I have remembered one of the staff of them had been shaking hands with us.
And one was [INAUDIBLE], engineer.
He was very good one.
He had-- he has a little finger.
He'd always touch others with his finger.
A special way, we-- we were having trouble.
And with this engineer-- Fox?
Fox?
Fox?
And it was the chief engineer.
And he-- I was for him, heir.
When he wanted to speak to me, he [INAUDIBLE], ask him.
So I have to answer and explain in Polish.
And I explained to him in Polish, and he would-- I understand the question, but I-- I won't have time to answer.
And I was-- not [INAUDIBLE].
After the war with-- when we were walking, he approached us, with my wife, and he tells me, what do you do now?
I told him, Mr.
Fox, sir, we have been living before the war, and now I will be able to try to help.
[INAUDIBLE] We [? haven't got ?] to fight.
So where did you go after liberation?
We had been living for quite a few weeks on the bunker on the factory.
My mother was very careful not to overeat us.
We had been all hungry.
She told me, eat slowly, not too much.
So you-- she probably had some experience, I don't know.
I couldn't tell you.
Anyhow, in a few weeks' time, the Russian prepared a whole set of-- of platform.
Without nothing.
Everybody can take with them whatever they have to put in.
We had been emigrated to-- to Poland, to Cracow.
You went back to Cracow?
Yeah.
Oh, many.
There was about, 200, 300 people who were on the train.
I couldn't tell you how many, because I was interested in my family, I couldn't care less who is going on.
But another people having by cart, horse cart.
You know, there was one party on that one, they had been doing.
When I come back, I met a [? chaplain ?] at the station who take me as my brother.
I didn't know him.
And he told me, ah, no, you have to go to my house, not far away.
And I told him, listen, I have to go to the city.
He told me, I can give you a lift to the bridge, but the bridge is broken.
You can pass the bridge, but I couldn't pass with a horse.
I want to find out-- I live with my wife, my-- my mother and sister over there.
I was the first to go to the city.
I want to find who is in the city.
So first of all, I went by train to the station, the last station in [?
Krovoja. ?] I was leaving my friends.
When I-- I know that they had been in-- living in the Polish Quarter during-- some of them-- the father and two sons and-- and a daughter and son-in-law.
And when I come over, they have a [? visit-- ?] everybody was over there.
So I was very happy to find out some of this is-- they were not-- a kind of family, very-- but we had been very close.
So the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE] take horse and cart and take us to the bridge, which was the monies.
Another chip, bring my wife and everybody from the other side of [INAUDIBLE].
So this way, we found a place to-- to live.
We had been living there for quite a few months.
Quite a few months.
Did you meet any of your old Polish friends or neighbors?
I will tell you, I haven't got very close friends, Polish.
I-- I know lots of them.
Just hello, sometimes we happen together at some social world, but not very close.

But the one-- one of the close friends of my wife, who was-- who has all the clothing, everything from Russia, and we had been selling it through the war, and there was-- he was bringing-- he was-- he was living in the same place before the war.
Next to-- on the first floor, which I was living on the third floor.
And his brother, his mother was very kind to us.
And-- You have to take the factory back, but you have to do it by-- by loan, you have to re-privatize-- re-privatize, yes?
Yeah.
No, no my mother's.
My-- my wife's factory.
Because there was, under the German, [INAUDIBLE].
And the-- the owners come back, and they have to be, under Polish government.
We have to prove that we are the-- the leader of the family, or something.
I think, quite a lot of money.
But by this time, I was selling cigarettes, anything what I can-- can make some money.
Oh.
For 15 years, till '59.
Yeah, till '59.
I haven't got some experience of this kind.
You know, how do you call it-- there was never a friendly one.
But because Jewish people, before the war, couldn't take any place in a-- in a police-- other-- so they had-- they had been quite well-educated.
But they couldn't get the place.
So after the war, most of them went to police.
We, in Cracow, didn't feel the anti-Semitism as much as the people from small town.
And that was the reason that the people have been taking advantage of the place that they had been taking.
And for-- for this reason, there was 400 people, [INAUDIBLE].
Three or four were Jewish.
They didn't think about the Polish ones, only the city Jews.
So there's always something what you can feel they are not friendly to you.
I will not tell you.
I find quite-- not as much as before the war.
But there was quite a-- because they had been coming from Russia, from camps and so on.
But not family.
There was one-- one member of the family who was looking for a family.
All of a sudden, there was coming another member of the family.
But it is very difficult to-- to say how many people.
And there was the Judenrat, coming to the Jewish people, who were supporting and some other was sick, like if you were-- medication.
And--
All the synagogues were left, but only one who was working was temple.
There was [INAUDIBLE] as well.
They had been taken from German, making some warehouse or something of this kind.
The old synagogue in [INAUDIBLE] was-- it's still a synagogue.
[INAUDIBLE] now.
But as I told you, I was not observant.
I mean, there was only one in case I have to go to-- to synagogue, with-- I had  a case with my [?
John. ?] He was playing-- there was a Polish kids around the house.
And one, he was in window.
And he call a friend, [NON-ENGLISH].
This means Jew.
I tell you, what kind of a joke it is?
You know that he's a Polish.
Then they would have a play, whoever see each other first, call Jew.
So I have to explain who was a Jew.
Who are the Jews?
So one, I have to tell him, who is a Jew, they're all the same.
And I have to take him to temple.
Because he never was.
He was going out with [? mates, ?] to the way he was coming to the church.
And he didn't know what he is.
I felt very badly about that one, because I was not, in that, mature that I have to give him some direction.
What year were your sons born?
Pardon?
And the other, '51.
I would tell them, first-- we have-- whenever we gather, whenever we gather, the friends, or so on, whenever we're talking, thinking, we're finished with the wartime.
And by comparison, our was paradise compared with those people who they have been talking about.
So I-- I have to be shut up.
What do you mean?
[INAUDIBLE]
No.
I was [INAUDIBLE] anymore.
The factory was closed.
I was swimming against the stream.
I was in private enterprise.
And we had been quite well-off in Poland.
But, as the time was flying, they had been screwing more and more and more.
You know, I-- I-- I will explain to you how it was.
[INAUDIBLE] Even I have all the documentation what I am producing, the prosecutor, I was in a parallel way with-- anytime he wanted to grab me, he can grab me.
So I had to tell them [INAUDIBLE].
We have decided to-- to go out from Poland.
And, [?
Isel, ?] you won't be finishing a trial.
Because it was, you know, you could feel the heat.
There was a time when I was going every week to Warsaw, with my products.
There was one case.
And I feel somebody following for quite a few weeks.
It take me lots of time and money to find out what-- what he want from me.
What do you want from me?
I find out that there was a practice for some young people who are coming and recruiting to the police.
What were you selling in Warsaw?
I was manufacturing, you know, after the war, whatever you were doing, you were selling.
I was manufacturing a fountain pen.
And a-- and a plastic [INAUDIBLE].
Pen.
Very little of this one.
But some fountain pens.
With-- but-- you know, there-- there was very big business.
Where I had been living with [INAUDIBLE] of Russia, Mr.
[INAUDIBLE], Mr.
Pennebaker ?], who was the owner of the factory, part owner of the factory of pens.
This-- this kind of pens, only--
And he told me, can you do something to-- to do, [INAUDIBLE], raw material?
I [? went over the ?] inspection.
I told him, listen, I don't know nothing about it.
Well, try.
We couldn't lose nothing.
And in a few months' time, with the advice of a few people, I started to-- to-- to manufacture it.
[INAUDIBLE] finish the raw material, with the right material, we have to substitute for something else.
And I found out that we can do it.
Yes, but I haven't-- I haven't been in-- in none of them.
It was too hard.
It was Mr.
Goebbels executed.
And another German was executed.
And quite a few Jew [INAUDIBLE] Dr.
Gross, medical practitioner.
[INAUDIBLE].
A Jewish policeman.
But quite a few Germans.
I couldn't [INAUDIBLE] I know, probably for sure, Mr.
Goebbels was executed.
And another German.
I couldn't tell you who.
But people know much more than I know.
Unfortunately, no.
Only one-- one [DRINKS] one time, I got a letter from my cousin who was living in Israel.
His name is Presser.
And he was in a swimming pool.
And his wife called him because he had some business.
Presser, Presser.
So she later was in the pool, [INAUDIBLE], who are the Pressers?
And he told me, he told me, I am Presser.
Do you have a family of Presser?
Yes.
And-- Bernard and [INAUDIBLE]?
Yes, they are living in Israel.
That was one-- was one contact.
Not with him, [INAUDIBLE].
The other contact was when they had been making-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taking money for his pacemaker.
He was sick.
And someone approached me to-- to give some money.
It was nothing.
Oh, that was quite a lot of time.
I couldn't tell you.
December, '59.
I chose Australia-- my-- my sister was first to go over there, to-- to here.
And-- and other people have been making permits for me-- for us.
But we had been, always, prolong the permit.
Prolong the permit.
Until the [? ambassador ?] tell us in writing that we would not arrive to Australia before 15 of December.
It was there.
Permit permanently.
Oh, there was gathering in art-- art gallery with plenty of Jews, was Mr.
Keneally was speaking.
I will tell you the truth.
The people who have informed him, fortunately, gave him quite good information.
But far away from the truth.
[LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] far from the truth, because they had only four months with us.
Or five months.
In a way, yes.
But, as-- as I would tell you, the book have to be written as a novel.
And facts, they have to put some material which [INAUDIBLE] not only facts, very dry.
Facts-- nothing.
What-- what There is-- I was working, I was helping, doing that one.
That's not interesting.
You have to-- the writer have to put it in such a way that people who hasn't been over there see the place and what he'd been doing.
And put it in a very-- way that-- that the people who hasn't been can feel what you had been going through with the [? one. ?]
No.
You ?] [? will ?] ask me why?
I will tell you.
We were coming to the place where the people who had been only five months with us have been sending one to the other one.
And I was a [INAUDIBLE].
Some people have been talking, why-- why didn't I approach him?
I would-- I couldn't approach [INAUDIBLE].
There was a case where these people have taken the administration places when-- who had been in Brinnlitz.
And who had been still producing people.
Worker.
Very difficult to tell you.
But I believe there's-- with the new coming, people [INAUDIBLE], there's about 20, maybe 25, maybe more.
Maybe 40.
I don't know.
Last year, we have a gathering.
Last year, yes?
There's an occasion or some kind and-- one of the [INAUDIBLE] arranging the gathering.
Kimberland ?] [?
Home, ?] what have you.
There was about-- I tell you-- that table and that table-- maybe 25, 30 people.
Yes.
The same story.
The film will not-- will not tell [INAUDIBLE] what-- what we feel or what we have been doing.
The film has to be done excited from-- from the people-- from the way Schindler was sitting on a horse over there, somewhere.
One of the true is when they were being expelled, and they expelled someone else.
And [INAUDIBLE] was very hot.
And it was true that he asked one soldier to put the water over the-- besides that one, [? whether things ?] are true, I couldn't tell you.
But, generally, I accepted that the film, that this expressed what-- what most of the people have been going through.
I am very happy.
Because of-- that's why I read to you, you know?
This-- one thing that you have to do, you have to be happy that somebody who was, when other people have the same opportunity to take the chances.
And didn't.
They have been very honest.
Even to this point that they have been taking some valuables from people who were working for them, and when they will be alive after the war, they will give them back, and they give them back.
But they will not take the chance, to take the risk, to save them.
Schindler has agreement with one of them that he take part of his foremans to-- to his factory to-- to Brinnitz.
But Schindler proposed [? him ?] to-- to do the same factory in Brinnlitz, he doesn't want it.
I'm finished, I believe.
I have nothing else to say.
